items.title: Regression vs Classification
items.description: Linear regression applies when the target y is continuous — the regression branch of supervised learning. Regression vs Classification defines problem type, output heads, and when to use numeric vs class targets; this topic implements the default continuous-y model.

items.title: Loss Functions (MSE)
items.description: OLS minimizes MSE, (y − ŷ)² averaged over examples. Loss Functions is the canonical home for the MSE formula and comparison to MAE; here MSE is the training objective for fitting w and b in ŷ = Xw + b.

items.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
items.description: Minimizing MSE on Gaussian noise targets y = Xw + ε is equivalent to MLE of w under homoscedastic Normal errors. MLE explains why squared error is the default objective for standard linear regression.

items.title: Gradient Descent
items.description: When the normal equation is costly or XᵀX is ill-conditioned, iterative gradient descent on J(w) = MSE finds the same OLS minimum. Gradient Descent covers step size, convergence, and SGD — applied here to the linear regression loss.

items.title: Logistic Regression
items.description: Logistic regression shares the word regression but solves classification via sigmoid/softmax and cross-entropy — not ŷ = Xw + b with MSE on continuous y. Compare problem type before picking the algorithm.
